old_claim.note: Urban Mass Transit Networks provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped signal system is installed at public street and road intersections, so roadways are the direct physical substrate being controlled.

source_shape.source_locator: Patent specification describes traffic through public streets and roads, signals at street intersections, and start/stop signals for intersecting roadways.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source locates the traffic-control system at street and road intersections, making roadways the controlled substrate.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Urban mass transit networks are one user/context of city streets, but the patented signal system controls road traffic at intersections, independent of a streetcar or transit network.

ontology_before: Traffic Signal Systems depended directly on urban_mass_transit_networks, implying transit networks were the direct substrate for the signal system.
ontology_after: Traffic Signal Systems depends on roads as the physical intersection substrate; transit networks remain contextual city traffic rather than a direct prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: traffic_signal_systems now depends on roads and no longer directly depends on urban_mass_transit_networks.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to streetcar or rail-transit traffic signaling rather than road-intersection signals.
A stronger source shows the first Cleveland signal was designed primarily as a mass-transit network controller.
The graph adds a dedicated urban_street_intersections node that should replace the broad roads edge.
short_reason: Road intersections are the direct substrate; mass transit networks were too narrow and contextual.
